name: B.S. in Computer Science and B.S. in Mathematical Sciences, Applied Mathematics

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: Within the visible terms the CS spine (CS 114 -> 280 -> 288 -> 350 -> 435/341) and the math spine (MATH 211 -> 222/335 -> capstone) progress sensibly, and a deepest prereq chain of 7 shows real layering. Two problems hold it to adequate: the parsed plan starts at 'Second Year - 1st Semester', so the entire first-year foundation (intro CS, Calc I/II, intro physics) is missing; and the shown plan is conspicuously physics-heavy (PHYS 234/335/430/432/442/485, OPSE 310) for a degree titled CS + Applied Mathematics, so the applied-math half is underrepresented in the visible plan of study.
dimensions.suggestion: Add the missing first-year term and explicitly label which courses satisfy the CS degree versus the Applied Mathematics degree so the dual-degree progression is legible end to end.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is deep and healthy: a deepest chain of 7 with a clean applied-math ladder (MATH 340 -> 391/331 -> 440/450 -> 451 at depth 7) and well-formed boolean/grade conditions throughout. The 8 dangling references (missingCourseCount 8: CHEM 126A, GER 200, GER 300, MATH 300, MATH 400, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A, PHYS 231A) are all lab co-sections or GER/elective-tier placeholders rather than genuine prerequisite breaks.
dimensions.suggestion: Map the 8 dangling codes to real catalog entries or explicitly mark GER 200/300 and MATH 300/400 as category placeholders so the graph resolves cleanly.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: The provided data contains no program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives, only a plan of study and course list. This absence is a real catalog finding and is especially costly for a dual degree, where the value proposition is the integrated computing-plus-applied-mathematics competency that goes unstated.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit program learning outcomes distinguishing the computing and applied-mathematics competencies the combined degree is designed to produce.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan does not reconcile: creditReconcileDelta is -33.0 (summedTermCredits 101 vs statedTotalCredits 134), driven by the parsed plan omitting the entire first-year term rather than by a curriculum flaw. As presented the credit distribution across GER, CS core, math, and physics cannot be verified, so this is primarily a data-consistency caveat that nonetheless undermines transparency.
dimensions.suggestion: Include the missing first-year term so summed credits reconcile to 134, then add a category-level breakdown (GER, CS core, applied-math, physics, electives).

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are labeled with per-term credit totals, but the page is incomplete and noisy: the first-year term is absent entirely (only 7 requirement groups, starting at Year 2), 'or ...' alternatives (or MATH 328, or COM 313, or R750 404) render as detached credit-less rows, and there is no program-level overview. The combination of a missing term and detached fragments makes the plan hard to read as a whole.
dimensions.suggestion: Restore the first-year term, fold 'or ...' alternatives into their parent requirements, and add an introductory program description.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The coursework is contemporary and marketable: CS 301 (data science), CS 351 (cybersecurity), CS 356 (networks), CS 435 (algorithms), CS 331 (databases), CS 490/491, plus a strong computational-math core (MATH 340 Applied Numerical Methods, MATH 391 Numerical Linear Algebra, MATH 453 High-Performance Numerical Computing, MATH 448 Stochastic Simulation). The chief gaps are a dedicated machine-learning course and no explicit optimization or cloud/DevOps exposure.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a machine-learning elective and a continuous-optimization course to convert the strong numerical/statistics base into the quantitative-ML skill set employers now expect.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are consistently present, specific, and substantive (CS 301 on data-science models, CS 341 on automata/computability, MATH 453 on state-of-the-art large-scale numerical algorithms, MATH 448 on stochastic simulation). A minor blemish is bleed-through in MATH 222's excerpt (which runs into unrelated 'MATH 225. Survey of Probability...' text).

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are generally clear with explicit grade conditions and sensible AND/OR logic (e.g., CS 341 '(CS 241 or MATH 226) and CS 280', MATH 450 'MATH 331 and MATH 337 and MATH 340'). Clarity is reduced by the 8 program-level dangling references and by 'or' alternatives appearing as detached null-code items, weakening end-to-end consistency.

missingTopics.rationale: The program has an unusually strong stats and linear-algebra base (MATH 333, MATH 337, MATH 391) plus CS 301 data science, yet no dedicated ML course; adding statistical/machine learning would be the single highest-leverage addition for an applied-math+CS graduate.

Optimization is foundational to applied mathematics, operations research, and ML training, but no optimization course appears despite the deep numerical suite (MATH 340/391/453); add a convex/numerical optimization course.

CS + applied math is a classic feeder to quantitative finance, and with MATH 448 Stochastic Simulation already present a mathematical-finance elective would open a high-demand career path currently unaddressed.

missingTopics.topic: Modern data engineering and big-data tooling
missingTopics.rationale: CS 301 is the only data course; graduates working with the large-scale numerical problems MATH 453 targets need distributed-data and pipeline skills, so a data-engineering elective is warranted.
Beyond CS 241/CS 435, there is no dedicated discrete-optimization or graph-theory course, a gap for applied-math students heading into networks, logistics, and OR.

emergingTrends.trend: Scientific machine learning and AI-augmented solvers
emergingTrends.rationale: ML surrogates and neural solvers are reshaping numerical computing; the program's numerical suite (MATH 340/391/440/453) is an ideal launchpad for a scientific-ML pathway.
MATH 453 already introduces HPC numerical algorithms; formalizing GPU/parallel computing would match where large-scale applied-math workloads now run.
With CS theory (CS 241/CS 341) plus deep applied math, the program is well placed to add quantum-algorithm content as the area matures.
Stochastic simulation (MATH 448) plus CS gives a strong quant foundation; ML-driven trading and risk modeling are a natural, marketable direction.
Combining the program's PDE/numerical courses with statistics points toward UQ, increasingly central to simulation-based science and engineering.

topSuggestions: Reconcile the credit plan by restoring the missing first-year term so summed credits meet the stated 134 (currently 101, delta -33), then publish a category-level credit breakdown.
Publish explicit program learning outcomes distinguishing the computing and applied-mathematics competencies of the combined degree.
Add a machine-learning elective and a continuous-optimization course to turn the strong numerical/statistics base into a modern quantitative-ML skill set.
Clarify the dual-degree structure by labeling which courses satisfy CS versus Applied Mathematics, since the visible plan reads as physics-heavy relative to the program title.

Fix presentation and resolve the 8 dangling references: fold 'or ...' alternatives into their parent rows, add a program overview, and map or flag CHEM 126A, PHYS 111A/121A/231A, GER 200/300, and MATH 300/400.
